exemplar.title: How is the influence of illegal fighting organisations on society of United States in the late 1900s portrayed in David Fincherâs Fight Club?

detailedExemplarData.title: How is the influence of illegal fighting organisations on society of United States in the late 1900s portrayed in David Fincherâs Fight Club?

subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: The research question is sharply focused and consistently anchors discussion throughout the essay.
Topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with clear purpose linked to English B Category 2.
The introduction and section headings maintain visibility of the investigationâs aim.
weaknesses: Methodology section is missing explicit justification and discussion of source selection.
Occasional drift into general social commentary weakens sustained focus.
Phrasing ambiguity (e.g., âlate 190â) creates minor scope confusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand evidence places two subâcriteria solidly in band 5 (topic communication and research Q) and one in band 3 (methodology). On balance, the work meets the lower end of the top band for Focus and Method, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Quotations from the film are well integrated and generally support key points.
Use of WHO statistics and CTE research adds credible external evidence.
Subject and category alignment clearly situates the investigation within required framework.
weaknesses: Some source applications are tangential (e.g., CliffsNotes treated uncritically).
Terminology usage is occasionally imprecise or colloquial, reducing consistency.
Contextual discussion of late-1990s U.S. remains largely anecdotal without deeper theoretical integration.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands consistently fall into band 3 (mostly appropriate application of sources, partial use of terminology, clear but partially developed knowledge). Thus 3/6 accurately reflects its mid-level performance.

strengths: Argument is coherent and sustained across social stability, psychology, and relationships dimensions.
Research draws on a reasonable spread of primary and secondary sources tied to the question.
Conclusion returns to the research question, showing logical progression.
weaknesses: Analysis often remains at surface level with plot summary dominating over deeper critique.
Limited evaluation of counter-arguments and some unsupported claims reduce incisiveness.
Reliance on some sources that post-date the 1990s underscores partial relevance to the period focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each CT strand sits in the 4â6 band (adequate research relevance, surface-level analysis, good but slightly inconsistent discussion). A score of 4/12 reflects the lower end of this band.

strengths: Comprehensive table of contents and clear section headings aid navigation.
Required formal elements (title page, word count, bibliography) are present and recognisable.
Layout is generally clear and readable.
weaknesses: Minor pagination glitches and inconsistent italics affect polish.
Some abrupt transitions between sections weaken flow.
Occasional line-break artefacts distract from formal presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the criteria for band 3: the structure and layout are appropriate and complete, with only minor flaws. Hence, 3/4 is justified.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is quoted verbatim, is sharply worded, and clearly anchors the essay in a single text, time-period and social phenomenon.  Most paragraphs explicitly return to âhowâ the influence is portrayed, showing good alignment between question and discussion.  Occasional side excursions into contemporary news articles and medical studies widen the scope slightly beyond the stated time frame, so the focus is clear but not always tightly maintained, meriting the lower mark in the highest band.

Paragraphs break down the influence of fight clubs on three social dimensions and usually move from example to interpretation.  However, analysis often stops at surface commentary; for instance, long plot summary is given where deeper exploration of cinematic techniques or sociological theory would be expected.  Logical progression exists but lacks depth, fitting the lower end of the 4-6 band.

Quotations from the film are plentiful and generally well linked to the point being made, and outside sources are inserted when discussing consumerism, crime statistics or brain trauma.  However, some references are tangential and a few citations (e.g., CliffsNotes) are used as authorities rather than being critically integrated.  Relevance is therefore âmostlyâ but not consistently clear.

Formal elementsâtitle, word count, table of contents, headings and a consistently styled reference listâare present.  In-text citations use a mix of parenthetical and time-stamp formats but are recognisable.  Overall layout is clear and readable even though some line-break artefacts and inconsistent italics remain, meriting the upper band.

The student demonstrates sound knowledge of the filmâs narrative and themes and links these to wider social ideas such as consumerism and rebellion.  Yet discussion of the late-1990s U.S. context remains largely anecdotal, and sociological or cinematic theory is only lightly touched upon.  Therefore the knowledge is clear but only partially developed.

The work follows the required EE structure: title page elements, abstract-style introduction, clear section headings, conclusion and bibliography.  Logical sequencing is maintained through numbered headings and a contents page.  Some pagination glitches and occasional orphan headings show minor flaws, so the structure is appropriate and complete but not polished.

The investigation draws on a reasonable spread of material â primary film evidence plus seven secondary sources â and most of it is clearly tied back to the research question.  Still, the range is fairly narrow (heavily dependent on the film) and some cited data post-date the 1990s, signalling only âadequateâ rather than âgoodâ or âexcellentâ research relevance.

The student states a precise cultural topic â the social effects of illegal fight clubs in late-1990s America as represented in Fincherâs film â and links it explicitly to English B Category 2 (culture and society).  Throughout the introduction and section headings the purpose (to explore social stability, psychology and relationships) remains visible, so the reader is never in doubt about what is being investigated.  Minor lapses of phrasing and occasional drift into general social commentary mean the focus is not maintained with full sophistication, so the work fits solidly but not perfectly within the top band.

The essay sustains an argument that fight clubs damage social stability, psychology and relationships, and the conclusion returns to the research question.  Nonetheless, evaluation of counter-arguments or limitations is brief, and some claims (e.g., unemployment rise) are asserted without evidence.  Reasoning is coherent but not fully incisive, corresponding to âgood with minor inconsistenciesâ.

The essay identifies the primary source (the film) and supplements it with half-a-dozen secondary articles (WHO, NY Times, Mayo Clinic, CliffsNotes, etc.).  Citations are present, but the student provides no explicit explanation of source selection, nor a methodological discussion of film analysis techniques.  Nevertheless, the range is more than minimal and shows some informed choice (medical study for CTE, newspaper articles on real fight clubs).  This places the work in the âmostly complete with some evidence of informed selectionâ level.

Key terms such as âconsumerism,â âalienation,â and âchronic traumatic encephalopathyâ are used correctly, and basic film-study language (scene, shot time stamp) appears.  Nonetheless, terminology is occasionally mis-used (e.g., âhumane psychologyâ) and academic register slips at points (âruns around like a headless chickenâ).  Consistency is therefore partial rather than full.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and correctly links headings to page numbers, enhancing navigation and structure.
Have you considered how Foucaultâs concept of power/resistance will inform your film analysis methodology? Clarify this linkage to strengthen your argument.
The transition from social stability to humane psychology (Section 3) is abrupt; add a bridging sentence to strengthen coherence.
Several URLs are broken across lines in the bibliography; ensure each entry is complete and follows your chosen citation format.
The topic statement uses âlate 190â which is ambiguous; specify âlate 1990sâ to align precisely with your scope and avoid reader confusion.
The subject and category alignment (âEnglish B, Category 2b â Culture and societyâ) is clear, effectively situating your investigation within the required framework.
Your research question is sharply worded but slightly exceeds the late-1990s boundary by citing modern studies later; tighten the question or limit sources to maintain focus.
The word count (3603) is within the 4000-word limit, demonstrating careful adherence to formatting requirements.
The introduction offers high-level theory from Foucault but lacks explicit connection to your film focus; anchor theoretical claims directly to Fight Club.
The discussion of U.S. organized crime feels detached; explicitly link these examples to the narrative and themes of Fight Club for greater relevance.
The focus on Fight Clubâs protagonist and split personality is clear and sets up your analysis effectively.
Introduce a methodology section explaining how you selected and analyzed your primary and secondary sources to demonstrate informed research design.
Section headings are well-structured, guiding the reader through each argument clearly.
The heavy reliance on plot summary in this paragraph limits your exploration of how fight clubs undermine social stability; focus more on analysis.
Integrate sociological or film-theory conceptsâsuch as anomie or mise-en-scÃ¨ne analysisâto deepen the discussion of social breakdown.
You cite contemporary NY Post and NY Times articles, but they post-date the late 1990s; ensure all secondary sources align with your period focus.
Critically evaluate the credibility of popular media sources (e.g., New York Post) rather than using them unexamined as evidence.
Listing news headlines descriptively does not fully explore their societal impact; follow with analysis of how these events reflect social anxiety.
Integration of WHO statistics on depression grounds your psychological analysis in credible data, enhancing argument validity.
The use of CliffsNotes without critique reduces source credibility; discuss its limitations and consider more scholarly film-studies references.
Consider applying a sociological concept (e.g., alienation theory) in the discussion of materialism to deepen your analysis of consumerism.
Use of CTE research from Psychological Medicine and Mayo Clinic adds a robust scientific dimension to your evaluation.
Clarify in your methodology how you identified and selected the CTE medical studies to justify their relevance.
The opening of the interpersonal relationships section lacks a smooth link from the prior discussion; consider adding a short summary sentence.
Inconsistent inline math tags (e.g., for time stamps) interrupt flow; replace with plain text timestamps to maintain readability.
Expand your conclusionâs discussion of limitations and counter-arguments to demonstrate higher-level critical engagement.
Bibliography entries display mixed formats; apply a consistent citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) throughout for coherence.
Avoid colloquial phrases (e.g., âto wreak an angerâ); use precise academic language to maintain formal tone.
The depiction of Marla Singerâs conflict effectively illustrates interpersonal breakdown through film dialogue selections.
